item.title: Customs breaks record with K1.19 billion collection
item.description: THE PNG Customs Services collected K1.19 billion from January to March 2026 – the first year it has surpassed the billion-kina mark in the first quarter, according to Chief commissioner David Towe. “Compared to the same period last year, revenue increased by K311.39 million – a 35 per cent growth – making it the strongest first quarter performance ever recorded by PNG Customs,” he said.
item.content:encoded:
THE PNG Customs Services collected K1.19 billion from January to March 2026 – the first year it has surpassed the billion-kina mark in the first quarter, according to Chief commissioner David Towe.

“Compared to the same period last year, revenue increased by K311.39 million – a 35 per cent growth – making it the strongest first quarter performance ever recorded by PNG Customs,” he said.

He explained that the amount was 25 per cent of the Government’s 2026 revenue target of K4.77 billion, placing Customs ahead of its year-to-date projection. Towe praised the work of Customs officers nationwide and reinforced the organisation’s responsibility to deliver its core mandates of border security and community protection, trade facilitation and revenue collection.

“My officers and I remain firmly committed to serving the Government of the day by upholding the law, protecting the border, facilitating legitimate trade and collecting the revenue that funds essential services for our people,” he said.

Towe said the Customs Corporate Plan 2024-2028 prioritised the modernisation of its administration, strengthened compliance, intelligence led risk management and fair, transparent enforcement.

“Our role is to ensure a level playing field for honest businesses while taking firm action against those who seek to undermine the law,” he said.

“Strong compliance protects revenue, supports fiscal discipline and strengthens confidence in the economy,” he said.

He also noted that PNG Customs’ compliance and reform efforts have again delivered record breaking financial results for the Government this year.

“This historic result demonstrates the effectiveness of disciplined compliance enforcement combined with efficient trade facilitation through ASYCUDA World, which is our automated digital system for cargo clearance and accounting, including the intelligence led, risk based operations,” Towe said.

“It shows that strong border control and modern digital systems can work together to support revenue growth without disrupting legitimate trade.”

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Sir Peter pledges K7 million
item.description: ENGA Governor Sir Peter Ipatas has committed K7 million of provincial funds to the Porgera-Paiela district to upgrade essential services such as roads, education, and health care. “We are investing K7 million, specifically for roads, schools, and health services in Porgera-Paiela,” he said.
item.content:encoded:
ENGA Governor Sir Peter Ipatas has committed K7 million of provincial funds to the Porgera-Paiela district to upgrade essential services such as roads, education, and health care.

“We are investing K7 million, specifically for roads, schools, and health services in Porgera-Paiela,” he said.

“Be responsible and take ownership of the government properties, and stop tribal fighting which has no good benefit but damage properties and takes lives.”

The commitment follows a series of consultative meetings in the district, where local leaders expressed the need for infrastructure repairs following recent disruptions in the valley.

Sir Peter said that while the Porgera mine’s restart was a big economic driver, the provincial government needed to take proactive steps to ensure that local communities realised the benefits.

Schools listed for refurbishment will receive classroom materials and staff housing upgrades.

“The reopening of Paiam hospital at the end of this year will relieve pressure on the Wabag General Hospital and the NPL Mine Medical Clinic, which have been the only available options for thousands of residents during the closure,” Sir Peter said.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Nomane tells PM: Stop meddling
item.description: OPPOSITION Leader James Nomane says it is the prime minister’s job to enforce accountability. Nomane said: “Whose job is it to enforce accountability? The PM!”
item.content:encoded:
OPPOSITION Leader James Nomane says it is the prime minister’s job to enforce accountability.

Nomane said: “Whose job is it to enforce accountability? The PM!”

He was commenting on the Government move to transfer the functions of the department of planning and monitoring to the National Monitoring and Coordination Authority (NMCA).

He said if enforcement driven by clear KPIs were not achieved, then heads must roll.

 “This is the result of leadership failure. Planning has become to scapegoat,” he said.

Nomane urged Marape to stop meddling with the Department of National Planning and Monitoring.

He suggests to him to apply the ‘trite principle’: “If it’s not broken, don’t fix it!”

 “Why are we meddling with the planning department to have it subsumed into the NMCA? This is not reform.

“There is a clear ulterior motive to promote control. This is sabotage.”

The move to separate the functions began last year at the launching of Reset PNG@50.

Marape, after demanding a report on the K56 billion spent in the six years of his term in office and not getting any, directed the separation of the monitoring and reporting powers from the department to the NMCA.

Nomane said yesterday: “Here is the truth: you cannot separate planning from monitoring and expect coherent development outcomes. One function designs the road; the other checks whether it was built. Split them, and you get two departments talking past each other while billions of kina in development expenditure drift without accountability.”

The lack of accountability led the PM to state at the Reset PNG@50 launch: “With over 10 million citizens to serve, every Kina matters. The K56 billion we’ve spent must translate into better services, not lost in bureaucracy, corruption or inefficiency.”

Nomane said the development budget was not an administrative abstraction.

“It is schools in Simbu, roads in Madang and clinics in Manus.

“When the institution managing that budget is destabilised, real communities pay the real price.

“Planning is the only department that understands the development challenges of the country and can translate this into action.

“Papua New Guinea does not need more departments. It needs fewer excuses and one government that plans, monitors, and delivers.”

He said the decision to split the DNPM would affect budget execution and would lead to a failed budget.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Madang registers 300-plus boats
item.description: SINCE starting its registration drive, the Madang Small Craft Registry has registered 334 vessels and collected K110,340 in revenue, according to acting registrar Captain Pascal Yali. He said that this reflected a growing community support in a province that relied heavily on sea travel.
item.content:encoded:
SINCE starting its registration drive, the Madang Small Craft Registry has registered 334 vessels and collected K110,340 in revenue, according to acting registrar Captain Pascal Yali.

He said that this reflected a growing community support in a province that relied heavily on sea travel.

He said most registrations came from the Rai Coast and Madang districts.

All funds collected have been deposited into the Madang government grant account to support maritime safety administration in the province.

The registry was established following Governor Ramsey Pariwa’s directive to activate the Small Craft Act 2011 through a provincial executive council (PEC) decision.

The legislation has existed for more than 15 years without any effective enforcement by previous administrations.

The Act regulates vessels under 10 metres in length, mandating registration, safety equipment and operating standards.

It also empowers provincial governments to enforce compliance, including imposing penalties for breaches such as overloading and operating without the correct safety equipment on board.

“It was only when the current Government appointed a board through a PEC decision last year that the Small Craft Board and Registry came into effect,” Yali said.

“The board then wasted no time in conducting awareness programmes, emphasising the need for small boat owners to register their vessels.”

Registration activities have been temporarily suspended this week to allow for critical governance initiatives, including the National Maritime Safety Authority inspector training today, Small Craft Board member inductions tomorrow, and a provincial small craft board meeting on Thursday.

During these sessions, the board would assess registration performance, address concerns raised by boat operators, review enforcement challenges, and finalise deployment plans before inspectors are sent to the outer districts.

Yali has urged unregistered boat owners to prepare necessary documents ahead of the resumption and expansion of registration activities, including a valid ID, proof of ownership such as a purchase receipt, and transfer paperwork including a signed ownership letter, a ward member’s support letter and a Statutory Declaration Form if the vessel was bought as a second-hand.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Warning out on Chiefs. Watson: Money alone won’t ensure success
item.description: A NATIONAL Rugby League (NRL) player, reported by Papua New Guinea and Australian media to be on the PNG Chiefs’ radar, has issued a strong warning to players considering a move to the expansion side. Speaking on NRL Monday with Andrew Moore on ABC Radio, Sydney Roosters utility Connor Watson said the “life-changing” tax-free contracts linked to the PNG franchise will attract attention but will no
item.content:encoded:
A NATIONAL Rugby League (NRL) player, reported by Papua New Guinea and Australian media to be on the PNG Chiefs’ radar, has issued a strong warning to players considering a move to the expansion side.

Speaking on NRL Monday with Andrew Moore on ABC Radio, Sydney Roosters utility Connor Watson said the “life-changing” tax-free contracts linked to the PNG franchise will attract attention but will not guarantee success or happiness.

“The decision must go beyond financial gain and focus on purpose, identity, and commitment,” Watson said. “It can be life-changing for you. But if you’re just going over there for the money, I don’t think you’re either going to last very long or enjoy your time very much.”

Watson said deeper motivation is key for anyone joining the Chiefs.

“You’ve got to make sure there’s also an understanding of who you’re playing for and why you’re doing it,” he said.

His comments reflect growing discussion about balancing financial incentives with cultural responsibility in the Chiefs project. Early speculation around the 19th NRL franchise, including talk of tax-free contracts, initially created excitement among players – particularly following the signing of Wests Tigers playmaker Jarome Luai.

Watson also acknowledged the competitive pressure the Chiefs may place on other expansion hopefuls such as the Perth Bears, with players weighing lifestyle, opportunity, and financial benefits.

While admitting that financial incentives will always play a role in recruitment, Watson stressed they cannot be the only reason for joining a club with such a strong national identity.

“If you’re going to go over there, you’ve got to understand who you’re playing for and why,” he said. “That’s what will make the difference between just being there and actually thriving there.”

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Gulf academy create history, book grand final
item.description: THE Gulf Academy Under-17 boys have created history in their first year in the PNG Rugby Football League Academy system, securing a place in the inaugural grand final after a strong 22-14 win over the New Guinea Islands Academy on Sunday at the Santos National Football Stadium. The new-comers showed classy football throughout the match and proved they are one of the rising teams in the competition
item.content:encoded:
THE Gulf Academy Under-17 boys have created history in their first year in the PNG Rugby Football League Academy system, securing a place in the inaugural grand final after a strong 22-14 win over the New Guinea Islands Academy on Sunday at the Santos National Football Stadium.

The new-comers showed classy football throughout the match and proved they are one of the rising teams in the competition despite only joining the academy programme this year.

Gulf Academy will now face Mt Hagen Academy in the U17 boys grand final this weekend in Port Moresby. Mt Hagen booked their place in the decider after edging Lae Academy 6-4 on Sunday at the National Sports Institute.

Assistant coach David Wena said the achievement was a proud moment for the province and the players after months of hard work and preparation. “I couldn’t be prouder of the results,” Wena said. “Big credit goes to the people who started this programme, especially PNG RFL Academy front runners Joey Grima and Raymond Aru.

“They went to Gulf and started this programme this year. It was our first time to be part of the academy system.” Wena said preparations for the season started late last year when officials travelled to Gulf to train local coaches, trainers and officials before player selections were made through school rugby league competitions.

He said the coaching staff used the knowledge and skills learned from the PNG RFL Academy programme.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Champions one step away from defending title
item.description: DEFENDING champions National Capital District Academy Open women are now one step away from another title after a dominant 18-4 victory over NGI Academy on Saturday at the Santos National Football Stadium. The win secured NCD Academy a place in the 2026 grand final after finishing as one of the top teams from Pool A, continuing their impressive run in the PNG Rugby Football League Academy competit
item.content:encoded:
DEFENDING champions National Capital District Academy Open women are now one step away from another title after a dominant 18-4 victory over NGI Academy on Saturday at the Santos National Football Stadium.

The win secured NCD Academy a place in the 2026 grand final after finishing as one of the top teams from Pool A, continuing their impressive run in the PNG Rugby Football League Academy competition.

Head coach Samuel Jackson praised the commitment and sacrifice shown by his players, saying the team continued to prove why they remain one of the strongest women’s sides in the competition.

“I’m so proud of the girls’ performance,” Jackson said. “They already proved their worth by winning the inaugural championship in 2024, and then they defended it in 2025.”

Jackson said reaching another grand final in 2026 showed the hard work and dedication the players had put into the programme over the past three years.

“Once again, we are going into another grand final after booking one of the spots from Pool A,” he said. “The girls have worked very hard and now they are trying to defend the cup one more time.”

Jackson also highlighted the many challenges the players continued to face away from rugby league, including school commitments and personal struggles, but said their determination had never changed.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Rise of a 17-year-old. Teen halfback eyes Kumuls future
item.description: At just 17 years old, Gulf Academy halfback Joseph Oraka is already dreaming big. The youngster, born of mixed Gulf and Central parentage, has quickly emerged as one of the most exciting prospects in the PNG Rugby Football League Academy pathway competition. His leadership helped Gulf Academy reach the Under-17 grand final in the club’s debut season.
item.content:encoded:
At just 17 years old, Gulf Academy halfback Joseph Oraka is already dreaming big.

The youngster, born of mixed Gulf and Central parentage, has quickly emerged as one of the most exciting prospects in the PNG Rugby Football League Academy pathway competition. His leadership helped Gulf Academy reach the Under-17 grand final in the club’s debut season.

For Oraka, the journey has been special. This year marks not only his first experience in the academy system but also Gulf Academy’s inaugural campaign.

“It’s really exciting for all of us,” Oraka told The National. “This is the first time for me, my teammates, and the club as a whole. We are thankful to the PNGRFL Academy team for giving us this opportunity.”

Gulf Academy booked their grand final spot after an impressive win over New Guinea Islands last weekend. Oraka credits the team’s success to unity and discipline.

“The good performance came because of teamwork,” he said. “The coaches have been emphasising teamwork since day one, and we believe in that.”

Although many young players dream of starring in the halves, Oraka originally saw himself as a winger. But this season, the coaching staff shifted him into the number seven jersey — a move he now fully embraces.

“My role model is Robert Derby,” he said. “I love playing on the wing, but this year the coaches put me at halfback and I’m loving it here.”

Like countless aspiring players across the country, Oraka carries big ambitions. His ultimate dream is to represent Papua New Guinea at the highest level and wear the famous Kumuls jersey. “My childhood dream is still there,” he said. “I want to represent PNG and play for the Kumuls or the Hunters.”

For now, his focus is on climbing the development pathway step by step. “My short-term goal is to make the Gulf Isou development squad and then, hopefully, debut in the Digicel ExxonMobil Cup,” he said.

“I know it’s tough, but I believe in myself. Discipline is key.”

Oraka also paid tribute to those who continue to support his journey.

“I want to thank my parents and family for supporting me and allowing me to play rugby league,” he said. “I also thank my coaches for seeing potential in me. I’ll do my best to make Gulf proud.”

This weekend, his attention turns to Gulf Academy’s historic Under-17 grand final against Mt Hagen Academy in Port Moresby — their first-ever grand final appearance.

“It’s going to be tough, but we are ready to rip in. Our focus now is on preparations,” he added.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: All must honour their fiduciary duties
item.description: LATE last year, at the launch of Reset PNG at 50, Prime Minister James Marape revealed that the Government had spent K56 billion between 2019 and 2025. Marape said: “In the last six years, under my watch, we have released K56 billion to provinces, districts, departments, provincial health authorities and tertiary institutions.
item.content:encoded:
LATE last year, at the launch of Reset PNG at 50, Prime Minister James Marape revealed that the Government had spent K56 billion between 2019 and 2025.

Marape said: “In the last six years, under my watch, we have released K56 billion to provinces, districts, departments, provincial health authorities and tertiary institutions.

This is not a small sum. Every leader, administrator, and public servant must ask: what have we done with it?”

” The money is being released, but are we seeing improvements in schools, hospitals and roads?”

The PM did not expect an answer back to his two questions.

The resounding, countrywide silence, goes to the heart of the lack of development that has gripped this country since Independence.

The reports we have seen, in the country’s own report on Medium Term Development Programmes (MTSP) I, II and III are a deplorable no show.

The sum of money committed for all three MTDPs together would go far beyond the PM’s K56 billion.

The negligence alone is staggering, never mind the bigger question of where the money has gone.

The PM’s demand is courageous but also foolish.

Courageous, because it would have to take a lot of courage to make such an admittance of an administration under your watch, and foolish because that is setting yourself up to be targeted by all your political opponents.

The full impact of the PM’s demand falls upon the Department of National Planning and Monitoring, the agency of government directly charged with planning, with programming, with evaluation and with reporting on development projects and funds across the years.

The department could not provide a comprehensive report on the PM’s demand so began a series of decisions and actions.

The National Monitoring and Coordination Authority (NMCA) was created by NEC decision 101/2025 and its second decision 16/2026 operationalised that body.

On April 24, Chief Secretary Ivan Pomaleu in a letter to National Planning and Monitoring secretary Koney Samuel, instructed the transfer of all functions, staffing, data, and funding for monitoring, evaluation and reporting responsibilities to the NMCA — giving him 30 days to complete the transfer.

Samuel has come back saying, short of taking over the full department, this request is difficult to meet in the time allocated because the functions proposed for separation are not stand alone ones as they are fully integrated into planning and programming.

Two hardworking, dedicated and very senior career public servants are in the centre of this tussle.

On the NMCA side is David Wereh, a man who rose up through the ranks to become the Secretary of Department of Works and Highways before he took up his current role as head of the newly created NMCA.

On the other is Samuel who spent 27 years straight after university in the department of planning, rising to the top finally.

They want to deliver the results that the Prime Minister demands but results in this game and in this country are variables that secretaries do not have full control over.

For that you need a systems wide search and here is where searching for results is like looking for the metaphorical needle in a haystack.

The money has gone out to provinces, to districts, to provincial health centres, and tertiary educational institutions.

All these centres have a fiduciary duty to report on how they have received and spent the public funds.

Unless they can carry out that duty, it is near impossible for anyone organisation to carry out that duty alone.

And there is only one way to force every institution to comply:

You control the flow of money. No reporting, no remitting of previous allocation, no new funds.

Only that is guaranteed to ensure there is proper, fair, accurate and timely reporting.

That is going to take a lot of gumption and it must come from heads of departments who are section 32 officers.

Of late we have seen too many administrators, heads of departments and agency heads who have given in to political pressure, who have become mere “yes men” or “yes women” to politicians.

Perhaps the PM’s parting shot should remind all: “If a minister or superior asks you to do something against the law, stand for what is right.

item.title: High cost of chasing luck
item.description: LET us be honest: betting –whether sports apps, pokies, or daily ‘lucky number’ tickets – is designed for you to lose. Bookmakers do not get rich by paying out more than they take in.
item.content:encoded:
LET us be honest: betting –whether sports apps, pokies, or daily ‘lucky number’ tickets – is designed for you to lose.

Bookmakers do not get rich by paying out more than they take in.

True investment grows value: a small trade store, a garden, a child’s school fees, a term deposit.

Betting builds nothing – just K5, K20, K50 burned chasing a feeling.

K20 a day is K600 a month, K7,200 a year – lost.

Worse, near-wins trick your brain like a real win.

Problem gambling rewires you, making you chase losses and forget priorities.

Families break, paychecks vanish, and stress grows.

Your money exists to protect your family and future.

It is hard to earn and far too easy to lose.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Port Moresby must clean up its act
item.description: IT seems that many podcasters and influencers from Australia are flocking to Port Moresby following the Papua New Guinea Chiefs signing Jarome Luai, a National Rugby League star. We are seeing social media posts discussing Port Moresby and some of us are concerned.
item.content:encoded:
IT seems that many podcasters and influencers from Australia are flocking to Port Moresby following the Papua New Guinea Chiefs signing Jarome Luai, a National Rugby League star.

We are seeing social media posts discussing Port Moresby and some of us are concerned.

As it is, the National Capital District Commission (NCDC) and Members of Parliament ought to wake up and start a clean-up campaign and ban the chewing of betel nut in public spaces.

The filth and unhealthy piling up of rubbish everywhere in Port Moresby will be photographed by these visiting persons and the images of the dirty face of the capital city will be shown all over Australia and New Zealand.

That will be bad for the image of the capital city.

That means, it will be bad for the city and possible recruitment of the star players who may want to come here with their families.

Safety on the streets is something that NCDC and the city’s MPs also ought to fix immediately.

It would be terrible if a PNG Chiefs player or a family member is walking down the street at Boroko, Waigani, or Gordon, and gets robbed.

The city authority and MPs must wake up from their sleep and start cleaning up the street, and make the streets safer.

The public transport in Port Moresby too is nowhere near those of other cities in the Pacific.

The buses are old, people smoke in the bus and spit betel nut juice out of the door or window as if they are still living in an uncivilised society.

The city authority and MPs in Port Moresby ought to wake up and fix up the city before more media personnel from Australia or New Zealand come up to report on the infrastructure and environment.

Additionally, the drains in Boroko, Waigani and Gordon are filled with rubbish and are really smelly.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Real win in UPNG-ANU partnership renewal
item.description: THIS University of Papua New Guinea–Australian National University (UPNG-ANU) partnership renewal is genuinely good news for Papua New Guinea. Too often, we celebrate announcements that never deliver.
item.content:encoded:
THIS University of Papua New Guinea–Australian National University (UPNG-ANU) partnership renewal is genuinely good news for Papua New Guinea.

Too often, we celebrate announcements that never deliver.

The UPNG and the ANU renewed their long-standing partnership, reaffirming a shared commitment to strengthening economics and public policy education in PNG.

In just 10 years, UPNG’s School of Business and Public Policy has grown from fewer than 200 students to 1,200, and from five courses to 25.

This is not just a press release achievement – it means classrooms filled, lecturers trained, and graduates stepping into Treasury, Planning, Internal Revenue Commission, and provincial administrations with skills the country urgently needs.

The human stories highlight the real impact.

Take Joanne Cecil from Milne Bay – dux of her school, selected for ANU’s Crawford Summer School.

She returned inspired, seeing PNG as part of a larger global community.

Education should stretch our best young people and send them home to lead.

This partnership achieves that, one student at a time. It is also built on a model that works.

ANU scholars teach at UPNG alongside local staff, while students study in Australia and return home.

Knowledge transfer happens locally, building lasting capacity.

PNG’s biggest deficit is not money, it’s human capital.

Partnerships like UPNG–ANU close that gap, preparing the economists and policymakers who will shape the nation’s future, from managing liquefied natural gas revenue to addressing climate change.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Neidab dedicates degree, award to late father
item.description: WHEN Neidab Ulu went on stage to receive his degree and award, he was oblivious to the cheering, applause and claps as he was missing and thinking of the person he wanted to be there to witness his achievement – his late father. “I lost my father during the first semester last year. He should have been attending and witnessing this ceremony.”
item.content:encoded:
WHEN Neidab Ulu went on stage to receive his degree and award, he was oblivious to the cheering, applause and claps as he was missing and thinking of the person he wanted to be there to witness his achievement – his late father.

“I lost my father during the first semester last year. He should have been attending and witnessing this ceremony.”

Neidab lost his father Ulu Rusini last year during the second semester of his final year – a critical time for any final year university student.

However, he managed to pull through and graduated with a Bachelor in Public Finance and Accountancy degree.

Neidab, 39, from Wanigela Village, Abau District, Central, is the second eldest of six children.

He was among those who graduated from the University of PNG School of Business and Public Policy last Thursday.

To his employer, the Public Service Commission, he was an investment which paid off when he not only graduated but also named the top performing student in the Bachelor of Public Finance and Accountancy class.

He received the Academic Excellence Award after being sponsored by the PSC, where he has worked for 11 years, to pursue the degree while working as the acting Senior Budget Officer and Certifying Officer.

It was an opportunity he took seriously.

“I thank my employer, the Public Service Commission, for the investment and opportunity and trust placed in me to send me for further studies.”

His work involves budget support, expenditure management, and financial advisory duties. He knows that the degree will help him in his work and lift his performance.

“What motivated me to go back for further studies was to enhance my skills in my current duties on the processes in place to manage the public funds effectively and efficiently.”

Neidab attained a Certificate in Business Studies in 2007 and a Diploma in Business Studies in 2008 at the Port Moresby Business College.

In 2023, he graduated with a Diploma in Accounting from the Somare Institute of Leadership and Governance (SILAG).

The loss of his father affected him. On top of that, he faced financial issues and tried his best to juggle his time between work, study, and family.

“I encountered financial challenges attending classes, attending to family matters, as well as work.”

At times when work and study demanded his attention most, he prioritised which one to tackle first.

“I’ve had clashes between my studies and my job. Some time, I had to attend to my work first and then my studies second because work is my bread and butter.”

The experience changed how he managed his time.

“It shaped me as a person to think critically and handle issues in a way that is balanced.”

At home, his wife Wendy Ulu and two sons Neidrolson who is in Grade Six, and Braden in Grade 4, endured nights when he had to burn the midnight oil.

Wife Wendy remembers those nights when they hardly went to sleep as Neidab would spend hours studying and keeping the lights on.

So she was happy and relieved to see him graduate with flying colours – a fitting reward for his hard work and perseverance. For the family, the hours of struggling to sleep are finally over.

Neidab dedicated the award to his employer, his late father, and his family.

“I dedicate the award to my employer and to my father who should have attended and witnessed this ceremony. And not forgetting my family who supported me throughout this journey.”

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: PNG’s frontier judges: peace-building from the bench
item.description: IN Papua New Guinea’s most conflict-torn provinces, an unlikely group of actors is stepping into the peacebuilding breach: resident National Court judges. Armed with an unusually expansive constitutional mandate, some of these judges are deploying their powers not just to process the aftermath of violence, but to try to prevent it.
item.content:encoded:
IN Papua New Guinea’s most conflict-torn provinces, an unlikely group of actors is stepping into the peacebuilding breach: resident National Court judges.

Armed with an unusually expansive constitutional mandate, some of these judges are deploying their powers not just to process the aftermath of violence, but to try to prevent it.

The scale of the challenge is hard to overstate. In Hela and Enga – two of the Highlands provinces hardest hit by inter-group conflict – sophisticated weaponry, warlord networks, and overwhelmed police have combined to produce a crisis that conventional state responses have struggled to contain.

Mobile squad deployments, special policing operations and draconian legislative announcements are all being experimented with.

But quietly, on the ground, judges are trying something different.

As reported in one of the daily newspapers on Feb 19, Justice Andrew Kostopolous, the resident National Court Judge in Hela, used a court-sanctioned inquiry into tribal fighting and lawlessness to confront the province’s deepening governance crisis head-on.

In proceedings marked by unusual candour, he issued bans on alcohol and the public carrying of bush knives, ordered the surrender of firearms, and named specific warlords as criminals subject to immediate arrest.

Justice Kostopolous also issued a human rights violation order against opposing factions in a tribal fight near Tari town, and delivered a stern warning that the province was “teetering on the edge of a constitutional crisis”.

“If the police aren’t doing their job, that’s a constitutional crisis,” he said.

“If the executive is not doing its job, that’s a constitutional crisis.”

These provisions transform PNG judges from passive arbiters into “social engineers”.

The Supreme Court has affirmed that the Constitution is to be interpreted broadly, as “goal-oriented, purposive” and “liberal”, not in a “strict, technical and legalistic” manner.

Judges are urged to “think expansively and to be dynamic” and to “use judicial ingenuity to do justice”.

This is an unusual mandate, though not a unique one: judges in South Africa and India operate under similarly interventionist frameworks.

Three constitutional provisions in PNG are central to this logic.

Section 57 empowers the Supreme and National Courts to protect guaranteed rights and freedoms – either on application or on their own initiative.

Under this provision judges have summoned police to investigate alleged rights breaches, order the closure of prisons on humanitarian grounds, and directed law enforcement commissioners on the management of their facilities.

Critically, Section 57(5) extends this power to cases of “actual or imminent infringement”, allowing courts to make anticipatory orders before a rights breach has fully materialised.

Section 22 grants courts the power to enforce constitutional duties and to formulate procedures and mechanisms where existing frameworks are insufficient.

Section 225 obliges state agencies and service providers to actively support constitutional bodies, including the courts themselves.

Taken together, these provisions transform PNG judges from passive arbiters into what legal scholar Bal Kama describes as “social engineers” – actors empowered to identify systemic failures and act to address them.

In Enga, resident judge Justice Graham Ellis has been equally active.

 Last month, he issued a series of orders on his own initiative aimed at stopping tribal fighting in the Laiagam area.

These included a ban on carrying machetes and axes in public, the establishment of police roadblocks in the Mulitaka area, and orders for police to arrest anyone breaching National Court directives.

He explicitly framed these interventions as a matter of human rights enforcement under Section 57 of the Constitution, noting that the fighting affected rights to life, freedom, protection of the law, freedom of assembly, and freedom of movement.

Justice Ellis’s reasoning is worth quoting at length, because it illuminates the distinctive logic that underpins these interventions: “In many parts of the world, judges can do no more than consider the effects of criminal conduct, notably conducting trials after it is alleged that a crime has been committed. However, the inclusion of human rights provisions in the Constitution of PNG enables a judge to take a more active role by making orders, where it is considered appropriate, that can try to stop and even prevent criminal conduct.”

Both judges are thinking systemically, not just case by case.

Rather than waiting for individual matters to come before them and then ruling on them in isolation, they are using the bench as a vantage point from which to map the conditions generating violence – and to issue orders aimed at disrupting them.

This is a direct challenge to what one judge has described to us as the conventional posture of the justice system: “We tend to come in after everything has happened.

“We wait until it has happened and then clean up the mess.”

item.title: Liberty Liko becomes fourth doctor in family
item.description: HER parents are doctors. Her brother is one too. And so is her cousin. So when it came to choosing a career, Liberty Liko needed little persuasion on what she wanted to be. “Medicine is all I have ever known. And since I was a little girl, I dreamt of becoming a doctor.”
item.content:encoded:
HER parents are doctors. Her brother is one too. And so is her cousin. So when it came to choosing a career, Liberty Liko needed little persuasion on what she wanted to be.

“Medicine is all I have ever known. And since I was a little girl, I dreamt of becoming a doctor.”

Her father is Dr Osbourne Liko, a surgeon and urologist, who also served as Health Secretary and Medical Board chairman.

Her mother Dr. Mary-Julian Baki is a pediatrician who had served in Gulf, Chimbu and Port Moresby for a total of 23 years. She is now in private practice.

Her brother Dr Malachi Liko graduated in 2020 with a Bachelor in Medicine, Bachelor in Surgery (MBBS) degree. He currently works as a general practitioner.

Her first cousin on her father’s side, Dr. Joyce Sonoling, is a psychiatrist.

On top of that, her family’s close friends include Dr Mathias Sapuri and his daughter Dr Yvonne Sapuri.

Liberty, 23, was among 400-plus students who graduated from the University of Papua New Guinea School of Medicine and Health Science last week.

She had her face painted in Kairuku to honor her Mum, who is the first from Waima in Kairuku to become a doctor.

Liberty wore a Morobe bilum and necklace to honor her Morobe heritage and father who is from Pindu in Finschaffen.

She dressed in the same way as her mother when she graduated from UPNG in 1992. It was to honour her and other women choosing the profession.

“Now that my dream is a reality, I am overwhelmed with gratitude, first and foremost, to our ever merciful God, and to my backbone, my parents.

“I am equally thankful to my entire support network as this achievement is just as much theirs as it is mine. I’ve never had to look far for good role models and I aspire to be even half the doctor my parents are.”

Her parents supported her education into university. She also secured a Government scholarship.

She attended primary school in Australia because of her mother’s overseas work attachment. She later attended the Port Moresby Grammar School.

She completed grades seven to 12 at the St Joseph International College in Port Moresby in 2019 before she was accepted into UPNG on an academic excellence scholarship in 2020. But Covid-19 affected her studies. So to get into medical school, she dropped the Science degree and withdrew from UPNG.

She enrolled at the Pacific Adventist University in 2021 for the Science Foundation with the aim of getting into the School of Medicine and Health Sciences. She attended the UPNG medical school from 2022 to 2025.

She will decide what branch of medicine she is to pursue once she completes her residency and internship.

Dad Dr Liko is proud of her achievement.

“Liberty earned her achievements on her own merits unequivocally. In fact, she had an enormous shoe to fill throughout her journey in pursuit of her medical career. Nearly every lecturer knew her, a majority of doctors and nurses knew who she was.

“But she has always been herself – level-headed, cool, calm and collective. She respects everybody despite any opinion they may have on her. She is always considerate of her less fortunate school mates whose parents are not in Port Moresby.

“She earned her MBBS through disciplined study, always have resilient study habits, perseverance and consistent hard work over five demanding years.

“Despite her family’s medical backgrounds and public roles, I’m proud to say that she never sought advantage or special treatment, choosing instead to uphold fairness and independence.”

Mum Dr Baki described Liberty as confident, determined and an intelligent young lady who always strives for perfection.

“She was no dainty girl growing up and always fought with her six older siblings to be heard. I am very proud of her as a mother and a female professional.”

But she knows well what lies ahead of Liberty.

“This is just the beginning of a long journey of sleepless nights, no weekends or public holidays, missing out on family events and sacrifice of personal time. It is a calling to serve.”

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.
